title: CC104 Week 1: C Foundations I
description: Learn what data structures and algorithms are, and write your first C program
congratsText: Great work! You now understand what data structures and algorithms are, and you've written and compiled your first C program.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Explain, in plain terms, what a data structure is and what an algorithm is
  • Explain why C is used to learn these concepts close to the machine
  • Write and compile a first C program (Hello World)
  • Understand the compile → run cycle
  • Recognize the 3 most common beginner compile errors

sections.title: What is a Data Structure?

quiz.question: Which everyday example best matches the idea of a 'data structure'?
options: A filing cabinet that organizes papers so you can find them fast
The act of searching through papers to find one name
A recipe that lists cooking steps in order
A calculator used to add two numbers

sections.lesson:
What is a Data Structure?
A data structure is an organized way to store information — like a filing cabinet, a row of lockers, or a contact list. It's 'the box' that holds your data.

sections.title: What is an Algorithm?

quiz.question: What is an algorithm, in relation to a data structure?
options: It is the same thing as a data structure
It is the steps used to work with the data in the structure (e.g. searching it)
It is only used for math problems, not real programs
It is a type of variable in C

sections.lesson:
What is an Algorithm?
An algorithm is the set of steps that uses a data structure to do something — like searching a contact list for a name. Data structure = the box. Algorithm = the steps to use the box.

sections.title: Your First C Program

quiz.question: What does this program do when it runs?
options: It displays 'Hello, World!' on the screen
It asks the user to type 'Hello, World!'
It stores 'Hello, World!' in a variable named main
It produces an error because printf is undefined

sections.lesson:
Your First C Program
#include <stdio.h> loads the tools needed for input/output. main() is where the program starts. printf() displays text on the screen.
#include int main() { printf("Hello, World!"); return 0; } // Output: Hello, World!

sections.title: The Compile → Run Cycle

quiz.question: What does 'compiling' a C program actually do?
options: It runs the program immediately without any translation
It translates your source code into a program the computer can execute
It deletes any variables you no longer need
It automatically fixes any typos in your code

sections.lesson:
The Compile → Run Cycle
Compiling is like translating a recipe written in English into a language the kitchen robot understands. The cycle is: source code → compiler → executable → run.

sections.title: Variables & Data Types

quiz.question: Which line correctly creates a variable that stores a whole number?
options: It stores the text 'age' as a value
It creates a variable named age and stores the whole number 20
It prints the number 20 to the screen
It causes an error since 'int' is not a valid word

sections.lesson:
Variables & Data Types
A variable is a named container that stores a value in memory. C requires you to state the type: int for whole numbers, float for decimals, char for a single character.
int age = 20; float grade = 95.5; char initial = 'A';

sections.title: printf and scanf

quiz.question: What is the difference between printf() and scanf() in C?
options: printf() reads input; scanf() displays output
printf() displays output; scanf() reads input from the user
They both only display output, never read input
scanf() is used only for whole numbers, printf() only for text

sections.lesson:
printf and scanf
printf() displays output on the screen. scanf() reads input the user types in. Together they let a program talk to the user.
int age; printf("Enter your age: "); scanf("%d", &age); printf("You are %d years old.", age);

sections.title: Reading Error Messages

quiz.question: This code is missing something. What kind of error will the compiler most likely report?
options: A logic error because the greeting is spelled wrong
A missing semicolon error after the printf() line
An error because printf() cannot be used in C
No error — the code will run exactly as shown

sections.lesson:
Reading Error Messages
Compile errors are common and normal — they're clues, not failures. The 3 most common beginner errors are: a missing semicolon, a missing bracket, and a misspelled function name (e.g. printf typed as pintf).

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: Which statement best summarizes today's session?
options: Data structures store information, algorithms act on it, and C lets us build both starting with variables and printf/scanf
C is only useful for advanced programmers and has no beginner use cases
Compiling and running a program are the exact same step
Data structures and algorithms are unrelated concepts in programming

sections.lesson:
Recap
A data structure organizes information; an algorithm is the steps that use it. In C, we start close to the machine — writing, compiling, and debugging a simple program using printf(), scanf(), and variables.
#include int main() { int age; printf("Enter your age: "); scanf("%d", &age); printf("You are %d years old.", age); return 0; }
